epigraph.text: The Lord is righteous in all his ways and kind in all his works.

content.text:
When a transversal crosses parallel lines, special angle relationships 
emerge. These relationships are among the most important in geometry, 
forming the foundation for proving many theorems about triangles and 
other polygons.

content.title: Corresponding Angles Postulate
content.content:
If two parallel lines are cut by a transversal, then corresponding 
angles are congruent.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This is a *postulate*—we accept it without proof. From it, we 
can prove many other results.

content.title: Alternate Interior Angles Theorem
content.content:
If two parallel lines are cut by a transversal, then alternate interior 
angles are congruent.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $\ell \parallel m$ with transversal $t$. Let $\angle 3$ and $\angle 6$ 
be alternate interior angles.

$\angle 3$ and $\angle 2$ are vertical angles, so $\angle 3 \cong \angle 2$.

$\angle 2$ and $\angle 6$ are corresponding angles, so by the Corresponding 
Angles Postulate, $\angle 2 \cong \angle 6$.

By the Transitive Property, $\angle 3 \cong \angle 6$.

content.title: Alternate Exterior Angles Theorem
content.content:
If two parallel lines are cut by a transversal, then alternate exterior 
angles are congruent.
content.label: Theorem

content.text: The proof is similar to the alternate interior angles theorem.

content.title: Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem
content.content:
If two parallel lines are cut by a transversal, then same-side interior 
angles are supplementary.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $\ell \parallel m$ with transversal $t$. Let $\angle 3$ and $\angle 5$ 
be same-side interior angles.

$\angle 3$ and $\angle 1$ form a linear pair, so they are supplementary: 
$m\angle 3 + m\angle 1 = 180°$.

$\angle 1$ and $\angle 5$ are corresponding angles. Since $\ell \parallel m$, 
$\angle 1 \cong \angle 5$, so $m\angle 1 = m\angle 5$.

By substitution: $m\angle 3 + m\angle 5 = 180°$.

Therefore, $\angle 3$ and $\angle 5$ are supplementary.

content.title: Using parallel line theorems
content.solution:
The angles $3x + 10$ and $5x - 30$ are alternate interior angles. 
Since $\ell \parallel m$, they are congruent:
\[
 3x + 10 = 5x - 30
\]
\[
 40 = 2x \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 20
\]

So the angle measure is $3(20) + 10 = 70°$.

The angles $5x - 30$ and $y$ are supplementary (linear pair):
\[
 70 + y = 180 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = 110°
\]

content.text: Summary of Parallel Line Angle Relationships

marginNotes.text:
Righteous are you, O Lord, and right 
are your rules.
